content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

December 7, 2017

Jason Canger, Esq.
Deputy County Counsel
P. O. Box 2415
Mammoth Lakes, CA 93546

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-205

Dear Mr. Canger:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the County of Mono regarding
Government Code Section 1090, et seq.¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the
conflict of interest provisions of Section 1090, not under other conflict of interest prohibitions, such
as Public Contract Code or common law conflicts of interest.

We are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71);
any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. Also, the Commission will
not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).) Therefore, nothing in this
letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any
conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions.

Regarding our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Mono County District
Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a written
response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, for
purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding against
any individual other than the requester." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTION

Does Section 1090 preclude Mono County from entering into an agreement for the
construction of a new County facility with HMC Architects? HMC previously contracted with the
Town of Mammoth Lakes to assess the office space and facilities needs of the Town and the County
(collectively, "Public Entities") in relation to the construction of a formerly proposed joint Town-
County facility.

¹ Government Code Sections 1090 through 1097.5. All statutory references are to the Government Code,
unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110
through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of
the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-205
Page No. 2

CONCLUSION

Yes. HMC produced the design concept for the formerly proposed joint Town-County facility, which the County now uses in its Request for Qualifications ("RFQ") in attracting qualified design-build teams for the construction of a new County facility on the same property in which construction of the joint Town-County facility was originally contemplated. In producing the design concept, it appears that HMC was acting on behalf of the Public Entities in making a determination regarding the Town-County facility and had significant influence over the County's decision relating to the construction contract for the new County facility. Accordingly, Section 1090 precludes the County from entering into the contract with HMC.

FACTS

You are a Deputy County Counsel for Mono County. In 2006, the Public Entities acquired a parcel of land in the Town ("Property"), which they continue to own jointly. The Public Entities' building leases are nearing the end of their terms and both have assessed their respective office space and facilities needs to accommodate future staff levels, functions, and programs. Specifically, the Public Entities retained the services of different professional architects to provide space planning and analytical services. The County contracted with Collaborative Design Studio for "architectural design and consulting services." The Town contracted with HMC Architects for "scoping and programming services."

Recently, the Public Entities discussed developing the Property together and constructing a joint facility. In furtherance of this idea, the Town's contract with HMC required HMC to consider and assess the needs of both Public Entities. Specifically, the Town's contract with HMC below called for the preparation of a space program for a joint civic center. Accordingly, the Town's contract tasked HMC to conduct a workshop with staff from both the Town and the County to determine both Public Entities' space and facility needs. For this reason, the County agreed to reimburse the Town for certain costs related to the Town's contract with HMC. HMC completed its Conceptual Design Study for Mono County & Town of Mammoth Lakes New Civic Center in May 2017.

As described, the contractual terms between the Town and HMC required HMC to:

• Conduct an intensive three-day charrette with key Town and County stakeholders to develop and validate program for the proposed New Town/County Hall Building, including determining space requirements and adjacencies as well as ensuring the initial site plan design and massing was appropriate.

• Administer a survey for Town Hall employees and prepare a preliminary space program for the New Town/County Hall Building, utilizing findings from the survey. Prepare a cost model that identifies the expected project square footage, given the Town's Project Budget, identified at $30 million.

• Prepare a Cost Plan identifying the expected cost of the project, given the expected square footage as identified in the preliminary program.

File No. A-17-205
Page No. 3

• Prepare and provide Meeting Minutes documenting all meetings during the charrette, a Bubble Diagram Floor Plan, a conceptual site plan, a conceptual three-dimensional massing study, and a Rough Order of Magnitude cost estimate.

• Provide cost estimating services for Planning/Conceptual and Schematic Design Phases.

• Prepare conceptual site plan(s), project schedule(s), and presentations for shared board presentation for the Town and the County.

At this time, the Public Entities are no longer pursuing a joint facility. Instead, the County plans to move forward to solely construct a new facility on the Property, while the Town has decided to delay its project for at least the next five years. In August 2017, the County issued an RFQ seeking to pre-qualify design-build teams to design and construct the new County facility. That RFQ process will result in the creation of a "short-list" of pre-qualified entities, which will then be eligible to submit proposals to design and build the new County facility.

In response to its RFQ, the County received four statements of qualifications ("SOQs"). One of the four respondents is a design-build team consisting of architects from HMC and a construction firm that would perform the "build" tasks of the design-build project.

The County is currently reviewing those SOQs and will ultimately exclude the least qualified of the four design-build teams. The County will then release a detailed request for proposals ("RFP") which outlines the project and the County's specific needs for a new facility. Each of the pre-qualified design-build teams may submit proposals in response. Prior to the County constructing a new facility, a lot line adjustment will be performed so that the facility detailed in the County RFP will be constructed on property owned solely by the County.

ANALYSIS

Section 1090:

Section 1090 provides, in part, that "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district, judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members."

Section 1090 is concerned with financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests, that prevent public officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their agencies. (Sigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is intended "not only to strike at actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of impropriety." (City of Imperial Beach v. Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.)

Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has a financial interest." (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) The prohibition applies

File No. A-17-205
Page No. 4

regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Id. at pp. 646-
649.)

Interpreting the term "employees" as used in Section 1090, the California Supreme Court
recently affirmed the long-standing principle that independent contractors are not categorically excluded
from Section 1090: "Liability under the statute can extend to independent contractors who have
duties to engage in or advise on public contracting." (People v. Superior Court (Sahlolbei) (2017) 3
Cal.5th 230, 233.) In Sahlolbei, the Supreme Court stated that "section 1090 liability extends only
to independent contractors who can be said to have been entrusted with 'transact[ing] on behalf of
the Government.'" (Id. at p. 240.) Previously, the Supreme Court has found that an independent
contractor for a state or local government agency that "has a hand in designing and developing the
plans and specifications for the project" is subject to Section 1090. (Davis v. Fresno Unified School
District (2015) 237 Cal.App.4th 261, 300-301.)

As an independent contractor, HMC performed a detailed Conceptual Design Study for the
formerly proposed joint facility. HMC was tasked with broad planning and management of the
Town-County facility including conducting stakeholder meetings and employee surveys to
determine the needs of the entities as well as designing and planning a facility to meet these needs.
Regardless of the fact that HMC contracted directly with the Town with respect to that formerly
proposed joint facility, the County reimbursed the Town for HMC's services because they
significantly benefitted the County. The contemplated new County facility detailed in the County
RFP will be constructed on the building wing solely owned by the County, but the construction of
the facility will be derived from HMC's design for the formerly proposed joint facility. In fact, the
County attached HMC's Conceptual Design Study for the formerly proposed joint facility as an
attachment to the RFQ for its proposed new County facility.

Accordingly, it appears that HMC was acting on the behalf of the Public Entities in making
a determination regarding the Town-County facility and had significant influence over the County's
decision relating to the construction contract for the new County facility. Under Section 1090, the
County is precluded from contracting with HMC to construct the proposed facility.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Assistant General Counsel

By: Ryan P. O'Connor
Counsel, Legal Division

RPOC:jgl

sections.question:
Does Section 1090 preclude Mono County from entering into an agreement for the 
construction of a new County facility with HMC Architects? HMC previously contracted with the 
Town of Mammoth Lakes to assess the office space and facilities needs of the Town and the County 
( collectively, "Public Entities") in relation to the construction of a formerly proposed joint Town­
County facility. 
1 Government Code Sections I 090 through I 097.5. ,
sections.conclusion:
Yes. HMC produced the design concept for the fonnerly proposed joint Town-County 
facility, which the County now uses in its Request for Qualifications ("RFQ") in attracting qualified

construction of the joint Town-County facility was originally contemplated. In producing the design 
concept, it appears that HMC was acting on behalf of the Public Entities in making a determination 
regarding the Town-County facility and had significant influence over the County's decision 
relating to the construction contract for the new County facility. Accordingly, Section I 090 
precludes the County from entering the contract with HMC.

sections.analysis:
Section 1090: 
Section 1090 provides, in part, that "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county, district, 
judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any contract 
made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are members."

that prevent public officials from exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering 
the best interests of their agencies. (Stigall v. Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) Section 1090 is 
intended "not only to strike at actual impropriety, but also to strike at the appearance of 
impropriety." (City of Imperial Beach v. Bailey (1980) 103 Cal.App.3d 191, 197.) 
Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest." (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates 
Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) The prohibition applies  
regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Id. at pp. 646-
649.)

recently affinned the long-standing rule that independent contractors are not categorically excluded

duties to engage in or advise on public contracting." (People v. Superior Court (Sahlolbei) (2017) 3

the Government."' (Id. at p. 240.) Previously, the Supreme Court has found that an independent

Regardless of the fact that HMC contracted directly with the Town with respect to that formerly 
proposed joint facility, the County reimbursed the Town for HM C's services because they
